text: Image quality assessment (IQA) is a basic yet challenging task in computer vision. While mathematical models can describe certain physical distortions like noise and blur in a mathematically clear manner, they are poorly described by natural language and fail to describe the more abstract feeling an image creates. To train a supervised model requires a large amount of annotated training data, which is typically very labor intensive to collect. Here, we show that using the visual-language priors learned by pre-trained Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) models, an untrained model can perform both the look and feel aspects of image quality assessment without requiring any fine-tuning for a specific task. Moreover, we show that using an antonym prompt pairing strategy for CLIP can help to make full use of the knowledge contained in the vector space of CLIP, while minimizing the ambiguity caused by the meaning of antonyms. We evaluate the performance of CLIP-IQA on a number of widely used databases that cover a wide range of perceptual and aesthetic distortions, and show that the features learned are closely related to the underlying perceptual priors.
headline: Fully Human Written
